data.title: Snowflake in spotlight as Wells Fargo survey shows uptick in consumption trends
data.description:
Snowflake (SNOW) was in the spotlight on Thursday after Wells Fargo said a recent survey showed an uptick in consumption trends at the data warehousing company.

“Our 4Q survey indicates an uptick in consumption trends among large customers vs. 3Q and improvement in future outlooks,” analyst Ryan MacWilliams wrote in a note to clients. “Views around AI skewed more positive, with qualitative commentary pointing to AI initiatives turning into greater SNOW spend.” MacWilliams has an Overweight rating and a $290 price target on Snowflake.

Delving deeper, some of the highlights of the survey include an uptick in indications for Snowflake-related spending growth of between 10% and 25% next year, which MacWilliams said was “the most positive read in 5 quarters.”

Other highlights include more indications of Snowflake being used for generative artificial intelligence strategies; and continued share gains, as customers who are also using competitor Databricks, intend to boost Snowflake-related spending over the next two years.

“We believe SNOW continues to look positioned to capture greater consumption from new AI workloads and grow within existing customers,” MacWilliams added.

Snowflake is set to report fourth-quarter results after the close of trading on Feb. 25. A consensus of analysts expects the firm to earn an adjusted $0.27 per share on $1.26B in revenue.

data.title: Deere soars to all-time high after beat-and-raise Q1 report, sees 2026 as ag cycle bottom
data.description:
Deere (DE) +13.2% in Thursday's trading to an all-time intraday high of $672.95 after easily topping expectations for FQ1 earnings and revenues, as profits in its construction business more than doubled, and issuing a bullish outlook for full-year sales growth.

The company reported Q1 net income of $656M, or $2.42/share, down from $869M, or $3.19/share, in the year-earlier quarter, but well ahead of the $2.02/share FactSet analyst consensus estimate, as revenues rose 13% Y/Y to $9.61B, sparked by a 34% jump in net sales for its construction and forestry division and 24% growth for its small agriculture and turf segment.

For FY 2026, raised its net income guidance to $4.5B-$5B from its previous outlook of $4B-$4.75B and above the $4.43B analyst consensus, and sees full-year net sales in the construction and forestry and small agriculture and turf segments to rise by ~15% each compared with its earlier forecast for a ~10% increase.

"We're encouraged by the ongoing recovery in demand within both the construction and small agriculture segments," CEO John May said. "These positive developments reinforce our belief that 2026 represents the bottom of the current cycle."

Deere (DE) said dealer inventory is in much better shape, with North American new large agriculture inventories aligned to retail demand, used large ag units down meaningfully from prior peaks, and small ag and turf field inventories running ~40% below last year, setting up cleaner channels and healthier replacement demand as the cycle turns.

While tariffs remain a $1.2B headwind in 2026, Deere (DE) has largely offset them through cost efficiencies and disciplined pricing, leaving the company roughly price-cost neutral for the year and preserving margin leverage if tariff relief or further mix improvement materialize.

Oppenheimer analyst Kristen Owen said Deere (DE) ended the quarter with relatively lean inventories, building historically less stock in Q4 and Q1 and leaving room for potential upside as inventory normalizes through the year.

data.title: Evergy outlines 6% to 8% plus annual EPS growth through 2030 as data center deals fuel transformative demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Evergy (EVRG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Campbell stated the company is "raising our long-term adjusted EPS growth target to 6% to 8% plus through 2030 off of our 2026 guidance midpoint of $4.24 per share." Campbell highlighted that EPS growth is expected to "exceed 8% annually beginning in 2028 and through 2030." He noted the outlook is supported by "the recent execution of electric service agreements for 4 data center projects."
  • Campbell reported $2.8 billion invested in infrastructure in 2025 to modernize the grid and replace aging equipment, while acknowledging "our financial results in 2025 were negatively impacted by weather and weak industrial demand throughout the year."
  • He announced the approval of large load power service (LLPS) tariffs in both Kansas and Missouri, establishing "a framework under which new large customers will pay a premium demand rate to locate in our service territories." The company received regulatory approvals for three new natural gas facilities and three solar farms totaling nearly 2,200 megawatts.
  • Campbell revealed new electric service agreements for "4 major data center projects," representing "1.9 gigawatts of steady-state peak demand" and nearly 20% increase in total peak system demand. He added, "We’ve included 1,300 megawatts in our retail load growth forecast in 2030, with the remainder ramping up after that year."
  • The CEO emphasized customer and shareholder protections in LLPS tariffs, requiring "minimum monthly bills regardless of usage shortfalls that cover no less than 80% of their contracted capacity at a premium demand rate," as well as termination fees and collateral requirements.
  • Campbell highlighted a 4% dividend increase to an annualized $2.78, with the payout ratio expected to decline over time to 50% to 60%.
  • CFO Bryan Buckler stated, “For the full year 2025, Evergy delivered adjusted earnings of $894 million or $3.83 per share compared to $878 million or $3.81 per share for the same period last year.” Buckler added that "early indications in 2026 are strong in comparison to 2025, and we expect to return to normal residential load growth in 2026."

Outlook

  • Evergy projects a 2026 EPS guidance midpoint of $4.24. Management reiterated "annual adjusted growth of 6% to 8% plus through 2030 off of our 2026 midpoint guidance of $4.24 per share."
  • Buckler explained the company expects "EPS growth to exceed 8% annually beginning in 2028 and through 2030" and "a retail load growth CAGR of approximately 6% through 2030."
  • The dividend payout ratio is targeted to reach 50% to 60% in the latter half of the plan period.
  • No equity issuances are planned in 2030, with the annual equity need from 2026 to 2029 forecasted at $700 million to $900 million.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted earnings for 2025 were $894 million or $3.83 per share, compared to $878 million or $3.81 per share for 2024.
  • Buckler detailed that 0.3% growth in weather-normalized demand drove a $0.04 per share margin increase. Recovery of regulated investments contributed $0.56 in EPS, while higher operation and maintenance, depreciation, and interest expense drove a $0.43 decrease.
  • Dilution from convertible notes led to a $0.05 decrease in EPS for 2025.
  • The company’s rolling 5-year investment plan totals approximately $21.6 billion from 2026 to 2030, a $4.1 billion increase over the prior plan, with rate base growth forecast at 11.5% annualized through 2030.

Q&A

  • Stephen D'Ambrisi, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about the absence of planned equity issuances in 2030 and future equity needs. Buckler responded that "no need for equity in 2030 because our FFO just greatly improves each year," but indicated more ESAs could require reevaluation of capital needs.
  • Paul Zimbardo, Jefferies LLC: Queried about the EPS CAGR. Campbell clarified, "The overall formulation, 6% to 8% plus... '26, '27 in the bottom half of the 6% to 8% range. For that, we expect to accelerate to exceed 8% annually beginning in 2028 then through the 2030 time frame."
  • Michael Sullivan, Wolfe Research: Asked about incremental load growth and CapEx. Buckler said the company uses "50-50 on debt equity funding of incremental capital," and Campbell noted that new ESAs would extend growth into the 2030s.
  • Paul Fremont, Ladenburg Thalmann: Inquired about industrial rates and cancellation provisions. Charles Caisley reported, "Our typical range is in the vicinity of $0.06 to $0.07 a kilowatt hour." Campbell explained, "the LLPS provisions are strong" and offer protections through premium rates and infrastructure recovery.
  • Anthony Crowdell, Mizuho: Asked about FFO to debt ratio. Buckler responded that "FFO to debt 2025 was right around that 14% area as well."
  • Ryan Levine, Citi: Sought information on alternative capital sources. Campbell responded, "the plan that we announced today is through the traditional financing mechanisms... For that next tier in our pipeline, we're absolutely open to and will be considering different paths."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts frequently focused on the sustainability of growth, equity funding, contract specifics, and credit metrics, with a tone that was largely positive but occasionally probing for more detail on risk protections and financial flexibility.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, with Campbell stating "we are committed to delivering against our objective of sound financial execution" and Buckler emphasizing "we have high confidence in this 2026 guidance."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted to more specific questions on capital needs and contract protections, while management’s tone grew more optimistic with the announcement of transformative data center agreements and raised growth targets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced a substantially higher EPS growth target (6% to 8% plus) and a step-up in projected rate base and capital investments, compared to more cautious forward-looking statements in the previous quarter.
  • Management updated on the successful signing of four major data center deals, transitioning from pipeline discussions to binding agreements, which was not present in the prior quarter.
  • Analysts in both quarters concentrated on growth, capital requirements, and regulatory issues, but the latest call featured more detail on the magnitude and timing of equity needs and protections in customer agreements.
  • Management’s confidence increased, with repeated emphasis on the transformative nature of the new data center contracts and their impact on future growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited negative impacts from weather and weak industrial demand in 2025, though early 2026 trends were described as strong.
  • The company embedded recent industrial load weakness in 2026 modeling and expects improvement as projects like Panasonic ramp up production.
  • Protections in LLPS tariffs and ESAs, such as minimum bills, creditworthiness standards, and termination fees, were discussed as mitigation against customer underperformance or cancellation.

Final Takeaway

Evergy’s leadership highlighted a transformative period ahead, setting a new long-term adjusted EPS growth target of 6% to 8% plus through 2030, underpinned by major data center agreements and a significant capital investment plan. The company emphasized robust protections in new large customer agreements and strong early signs for 2026 demand, projecting accelerating growth and improved financial flexibility while maintaining a focus on affordability and reliability for existing customers.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Delinquency rates deteriorate, charge-offs mixed: January Credit Pulse
data.description:
Credit card trusts saw  delinquency rates rise modestly in January for each of the seven banks tracked by Seeking Alpha in its monthly roundup, while net charge-off rates were a mixed lot. Taking a long view, though, the delinquency and charge-off numbers still appear stronger than the metrics from January 2020, before the pandemic roiled the economy.

Either consumers paid down some debt or credit card lenders pulled back on lending last month. Total loans from the seven trusts were $521.4B, down 2.3% from December's total. 

The average delinquency rate in January increased to 2.81% from 2.75% in December and from 2.58% a year ago.

The average net charge-off rate of 3.53% rose from 3.27% in the prior month and dropped from 4.11% in January 2025. Bread Holdings (BFH), though, can skew the average because its net charge-off rate is significantly higher than the others. Excluding Bread, the average net charge-off rate was 2.94%, down from 3.13% in December and 3.50% in January 2025.

American Express (AXP), Synchrony Financial (SYF), Citigroup (C), and Bread Financial (BFH) saw their net charge-off rates decline month-over-month, while Capital One (COF), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), and Bank of America (BAC) saw net charge-off rates rise.

Total delinquencies for J.P. Morgan's Trust Tracker Index rose by 5 basis points M/M to 1.28%, in line with J.P. Morgan analyst Richard Shane's expectations. Net charge-offs for the index improved by 11 bps M/M to 1.84%, "modestly outperforming JPMe of 1.92%," the analyst noted.

data.title: Reddit tests new AI search feature to show shopping suggestions
data.description:
Reddit (RDDT) said Thursday it is testing a new AI-powered search feature that will show product recommendations with a small group of U.S.-based users.

The company said it is testing a new product carousel feature that will be displayed at the bottom of a search result when the query is related to household electronics.

The product carousels will also mention pricing, images, and direct where-to-buy links.

The early test feature will appear to those Redditors in the U.S. who submit shopping-related queries on electronic items.

"This feature surfaces top-recommended products directly from discussions, giving Redditors instant information about any product," Reddit said.

data.title: ONE Gas outlines 2026 adjusted EPS guidance of $4.83–$4.95 while expanding system capacity and reinforcing Texas regulatory impact
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ONE Gas (OGS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Robert McAnnally, President and CEO, opened by highlighting the company's operational resilience during Winter Storm Fern, stating, "On the peak day of the storm, we delivered over 3 billion cubic feet of gas to our customers with no supply disruptions." He credited post-2021 investments, including the Austin system reinforcement and increased storage capacity, for enhancing winter peak capacity by approximately 25%. The CEO also noted, "Our full year 2025 financial results were also strong," referencing the company’s performance in line with raised EPS guidance and a continued record of meeting or surpassing the midpoint of initial EPS guidance for 12 consecutive years.
  • McAnnally announced the introduction of a non-GAAP adjustment to net income and EPS to reflect Texas House Bill 4384's impact, stating this provides "clarity to our disclosures and helps better illustrate the earnings that our regulator allows."
  • Christopher Sighinolfi, Senior VP & CFO, reported, "2025 net income totaled $264 million or $4.37 per diluted share compared with $223 million and $3.91 in 2024. Capital expenditures totaled $760 million for the year."
  • Sighinolfi detailed the expanded impact of Texas House Bill 4384, which "widened to nearly $7 million or roughly $0.11 per diluted share" in 2025 and is anticipated to represent a $12 million variance in 2026. He stated, "We will report non-GAAP adjusted net income and EPS figures as key indicators of business performance going forward."
  • Curtis Dinan, Senior VP & COO, provided a regulatory update: "Earlier this month, we received a final order in the Texas rate case. The Railroad Commission approved a $14.4 million revenue increase, a 9.8% return on equity and a 59.9% equity ratio." Dinan also announced his expanded role as President and COO.

Outlook

  • Sighinolfi stated, "For 2026, we expect adjusted net income in the range of $306 million to $314 million and adjusted earnings per share in the range of $4.83 to $4.95."
  • The company continues to expect long-term adjusted net income growth of 7% to 9% and adjusted EPS growth of 5% to 7%, using adjusted 2025 actual results as the baseline for 2026–2030, with an implied 2030 adjusted EPS midpoint of roughly $6.
  • Sighinolfi confirmed, "Our expected financial performance, as expressed in our 2026 financial guidance, has not changed, but we intend to also provide guidance based on our adjusted numbers going forward."

Financial Results

  • Adjusted net income for Q4 was $90 million or $1.48 per diluted share compared with $78 million and $1.35 in the same period in 2024. Full year adjusted net income was $271 million or $4.48 per diluted share compared with $225 million and $3.94 in 2024.
  • Operating and maintenance expense for the full year was up approximately 5% over 2024, slightly above the 4% CAGR guidance. Sighinolfi attributed this to executing projects earlier than initially planned.
  • Interest expense in the quarter was $2.9 million lower year-over-year, primarily reflecting lower rates on commercial paper borrowings and the implementation of Texas House Bill 4384.
  • The balance sheet remains strong, with S&P affirming its A- rating and Moody's affirming an A3 rating, both with stable outlooks.

Q&A

  • Gabriel Moreen, Mizuho Securities: Asked about the timing and materiality of non-GAAP adjustments, and whether this impacts equity issuance or capital structure. Sighinolfi responded, "It really doesn't have an effect in a meaningful way on that... The house bill accounting treatment is in the early part of it, more impactful to earnings than it is to cash flow."
  • Moreen followed up on growth opportunities and competitive landscape for new projects. Dinan explained, "We have lots of these opportunities coming towards us... where those 2 things are maybe equal between a midstream provider and us, the tiebreaker often is our regulatory structure and being able to be very transparent."
  • Paul Zimbardo, Jefferies: Queried the potential benefit from proposed Kansas legislation. Dinan indicated the bill is in early stages, with the cap per customer increasing from $0.80 to $1.35, and clarified, "Currently, what qualifies is safety-related expenditures... this would add in any growth capital we're doing facilities that we're putting in place."
  • David Arcaro, Morgan Stanley: Asked if the latest Texas rate case outcome is embedded in guidance. Sighinolfi confirmed, "Yes, it does," adding that the accrual and deferral is "not" cash, but once rolled into the GRIP filing, "it will be a larger cash flow item."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions were detailed, focused on regulatory adjustments, growth opportunities, and capital impacts, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone and seeking clarity on new accounting treatments and legislative benefits.
  • Management maintained a confident and transparent tone, using phrases such as "we feel very confident" and "we are focused on efficient execution," while directly addressing analyst inquiries about regulatory and financial impacts.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained steady, with an incremental emphasis on transparency regarding non-GAAP adjustments and regulatory changes. Analysts continued to probe for clarity on legislative impacts and growth trajectory, maintaining a similar tone to last quarter.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company shifted from discussing tightening earnings guidance in Q3 to providing explicit 2026 adjusted EPS guidance and long-term outlooks in Q4, reflecting the expanded use of non-GAAP measures due to new Texas legislation.
  • Strategic focus evolved from project execution and in-sourcing operational initiatives in Q3 to emphasizing the impact of regulatory changes and legislative developments in Q4.
  • Analysts maintained their focus on growth opportunities, legislative impacts, and financial clarity, with a slight increase in questions about regulatory accounting and its influence on forward guidance.
  • Management’s confidence remained high, with continued commitment to disciplined capital execution and transparent disclosure of regulatory impacts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted the widening delta between regulatory and GAAP accounting due to Texas House Bill 4384 and noted that this difference is now "a fundamental aspect of our regulatory framework."
  • O&M expenses were slightly above guidance due to project timing, but management positioned this as an efficiency measure.
  • Analysts expressed interest in potential impacts of legislative changes in both Texas and Kansas on capital recovery and earnings, as well as on future rate cases and cash flow timing.

Final Takeaway

ONE Gas emphasized operational excellence during severe weather, continued disciplined capital investment, and proactive regulatory management. The company introduced non-GAAP adjusted measures to capture the growing impact of Texas House Bill 4384 on earnings, with adjusted EPS guidance of $4.83 to $4.95 for 2026 and long-term adjusted EPS growth targets of 5% to 7%. Strategic expansion projects and evolving regulatory environments in Texas and Kansas are expected to underpin growth and support the company’s commitment to reliability, affordability, and shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Insmed outlines $1B-plus BRINSUPRI revenue target for 2026 while advancing pipeline and ARIKAYCE expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Insmed Incorporated (INSM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • William Lewis, President, CEO & Chairman, opened by stating 2025 was an "exceptional year for Insmed, defined by extraordinary execution and transformative impact" driven by the approval and launch of BRINSUPRI and continued performance of ARIKAYCE. He highlighted, "we witnessed the approval of BRINSUPRI and its stunning early months of launch performance as well as the continued global performance of ARIKAYCE, which showed significant acceleration from commercial efforts in Europe and especially Japan." The company completed the acquisition of INS1148 and expanded its clinical pipeline despite discontinuing the CRS Without Nasal Polyps program last quarter.
  • Lewis announced, "Today, we are pleased to announce revenue guidance for BRINSUPRI of at least $1 billion in 2026." He connected this to broader company targets, noting, "we anticipate total company revenue in 2026 to be more than double the revenue we produced in 2025."
  • He emphasized BRINSUPRI's growth opportunity, referencing a total addressable market of 500,000 diagnosed U.S. patients and less than 5% penetration to date, and described the potential to expand further by identifying undiagnosed bronchiectasis within 32 million COPD or asthma patients. "As we're able to identify patients that may benefit from BRINSUPRI from within this broader population, we have a chance to help physicians deliver what we believe could be a game-changing medicine."
  • On ARIKAYCE, Lewis stated, "Japan had a particularly impressive 2025, delivering 40% growth compared to 2024 and contributing more than 1/4 of ARIKAYCE's global revenues. In Europe, ARIKAYCE grew even faster, albeit from a more modest revenue base." He expects the upcoming Phase III ENCORE trial to potentially expand ARIKAYCE's addressable market.
  • Lewis also reported, "Last month, we were informed by the FDA's Office of Orphan Drug Products Development of their decision to grant orphan drug designation to treprostinil palmitil for the treatment of pulmonary arterial hypertension," reflecting progress for TPIP.
  • Sara Bonstein, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "we are now positioned to provide a gross-to-net guidance range of mid-20s to low 30s for BRINSUPRI in 2026. Importantly, our 2025 actual gross-to-net for BRINSUPRI was also in that range." She detailed a cash position of approximately $1.4 billion at year-end, and emphasized, "I'm confident we can achieve cash flow positivity without needing to access additional capital."

Outlook

  • The company projects BRINSUPRI revenue of at least $1 billion in 2026 and total company revenue to be more than double 2025's revenue, supported by "additional visibility we have gained into the market access environment and the early performance we have seen from BRINSUPRI so far this year" (Lewis).
  • Bonstein guided BRINSUPRI gross-to-net for 2026 in the "mid-20s to low 30s" range and ARIKAYCE gross-to-net in the "low to mid-20s," slightly higher than 2025.
  • Management reiterated confidence in achieving cash flow positivity without additional capital, though they may choose to raise capital for business development opportunities.

Financial Results

  • BRINSUPRI generated $144.6 million in net revenue in its first full quarter. Lewis described this as "exceeding that bar" set by comparable respiratory launches.
  • Cost of product revenues for Q4 2025 was $44.2 million, or 16.8% of revenues. Bonstein noted this was "lower on a percentage basis based on our historical performance, reflecting the positive contributions of BRINSUPRI to the company's gross margin profile."
  • Cash burn in Q4 included approximately $70 million of one-time items, attributed mainly to the acquisition of INS1148 and a milestone payment to AstraZeneca. The underlying cash burn was similar to the prior quarter.

Q&A

  • Joseph Schwartz, Leerink Partners, asked about plans to develop other DPP1 inhibitors. Lewis replied, "Absolutely, we intend to bring other DPP1s forward, not just in the respiratory indications, but in others," including rheumatoid arthritis and irritable bowel disease.
  • Jason Zemansky, BofA Securities, asked about factors supporting BRINSUPRI guidance. Lewis responded, "across all the metrics, we're seeing at or above our targets... we feel very, very good about where things are."
  • Olivia Brayer, Cantor, inquired about script trends and BD activity. Lewis said, "things just are very strong out of the gate," and outlined interest in adding "first or best-in-class compounds" to the pipeline.
  • Ritu Baral, TD Cowen, questioned guidance refinement and documentation requirements. Lewis replied, "The market access picture is fantastic from our point of view. It's ahead of where we thought it was going to be internally."
  • Gavin Clark-Gartner, Evercore ISI, asked about approval rates and conversion speed. Lewis said, "our benchmark internally... is very high, and we are at or exceeding that level."
  • Additional questions covered ex-U.S. contributions, business development, prescribing depth, market access, and ARIKAYCE's upcoming ENCORE trial.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was generally positive, with repeated congratulations on the quarter and confidence in the launch trajectory. Multiple questions probed for risks but were met with confident responses.
  • Management's prepared remarks and answers were highly confident and assertive, frequently emphasizing that "we feel very good about the way this launch is going" and "we are incredibly happy about where we are and where we're going."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management have shifted from cautious optimism to assertive confidence, supported by tangible launch metrics and revenue guidance. There were few signs of defensiveness or hesitation from management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company moved from early launch caution and limited data in Q3 to providing explicit revenue guidance in Q4, citing strong execution and accelerated uptake of BRINSUPRI.
  • Previous quarter focused on breadth of prescribing and initial market access; current quarter highlights both depth and acceleration, with a strong push towards expanding total addressable market.
  • Strategic focus has shifted to aggressive growth, pipeline expansion, and leveraging positive payer dynamics, with a new emphasis on identifying undiagnosed patients in COPD and asthma populations.
  • Guidance language evolved from references to launch analogs to explicit, ambitious targets and expectations for company-wide revenue and profitability.
  • Analysts in Q3 were probing for early feedback and caution; in Q4, questions focused on sustainability, expansion, and further upside.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged potential variability in BRINSUPRI launch trajectory and noted, "the shape of the launch from one month to another can be inherently variable."
  • Payer access and documentation requirements were discussed as manageable, with Lewis stating, "we have seen a very high payer approval rate so far, even for payers requiring documentation."
  • Potential need for additional capital is not tied to current operations but to business development opportunities, with Bonstein reiterating, "we may choose to source additional capital to advance and expand our pipeline."
  • Ex-U.S. launch of BRINSUPRI is on hold pending clarity on MFN policies, limiting international contribution in 2026.

Final Takeaway

Insmed's Q4 2025 call positions the company for transformative growth, with BRINSUPRI's launch delivering robust early revenue and setting a $1 billion-plus target for 2026. Supported by expanding market access, positive feedback from physicians and patients, and potential upside from undiagnosed populations, the company expects to more than double revenue year-over-year while advancing its pipeline. Strategic discipline, confidence in achieving cash flow positivity, and a focus on high-value expansion opportunities define Insmed's outlook as it enters 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Texas Pacific Land targets data center and desalination growth while setting water sales record in Q4 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Tyler Glover, President, CEO & Trustee, highlighted an "excellent finish to 2025 with quarterly records set for oil and gas royalty production, water sales volumes and produced water royalties." Glover noted, "Water sales volumes this quarter exceeded 1 million barrels per day for the first time in our history, growing 36% year-over-year and produced water royalty volumes grew 22% year-over-year."
  • The company set annual records for revenue, net income, and free cash flow in fiscal year 2025, despite a decline in oil prices from $95 per barrel in 2022 to $65 per barrel in 2025. Glover attributed countercyclical growth to "continued market capture, talented employees, commercial development focus, differentiated scale across royalties, land and water and self-funded acquisitions and investments."
  • Management announced a strategic investment in Bolt Data & Energy, describing Bolt as "a new AI infrastructure platform chaired by former Google CEO, Eric Schmidt." TPL retains a right of first refusal to provide water to Bolt-affiliated projects and is working on several data center developments, with some conversations "in advanced stages of planning."
  • Glover provided an update on the 10,000 barrel per day R&D desalination facility in Orla, Texas, saying it is "nearing completion" and now expected to begin taking produced water in the coming months. The company plans to invest approximately $20 million in co-location equipment to evaluate waste heat capture and data center cooling at the facility.
  • Glover concluded, "With our industry-leading margins, our fortress balance sheet and a $500 million undrawn credit facility, not only can we tolerate periods of low commodity prices, we have considerable capability to invest opportunistically as we look to consolidate high-quality assets and expand market capture."
  • Chris Steddum, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "Consolidated revenues during the fourth quarter of 2025 were approximately $212 million. Consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $178 million. Adjusted EBITDA margin was 84% and free cash flow was $119 million."
  • Steddum added, "Yesterday, we announced a regular dividend of $0.60 per share, which represents a 12.5% increase versus the prior quarter dividend."

Outlook

  • For 2026, TPL plans capital expenditures of approximately $65 million to $75 million, with $20 million allocated to waste heat capture and data center and power generation co-location potential for its freeze desalination facility. The remainder will focus on water sales business improvements and maintenance.
  • Glover stated the company expects to "commence operations and ramping volumes on our Orla desalination facility, evaluating the potential to bring large-scale freeze desalination to the Permian."
  • Management expressed confidence in "multiple new updates to share before the end of the year" regarding data center developments.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter consolidated revenues were approximately $212 million, consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $178 million, and free cash flow was $119 million. Full-year free cash flow reached approximately $498 million, an 8% year-over-year increase.
  • Steddum reported, "Of the approximately 34,600 barrels of oil equivalent per day for full year 2025 royalty production, the acquisition that closed last November contributed approximately 500 barrels of oil equivalent per day."
  • The company exited the year with $145 million of cash and no debt, and its $500 million credit facility remains fully undrawn.
  • Capital expenditures last year were $66 million, including $6 million of payables, at the low end of original guidance.
  • Steddum noted, "Adjusted EBITDA margin was 84%."

Q&A

  • Derrick Whitfield, Texas Capital Securities: Asked about the evolving opportunity set for power and data center development. Glover responded, "The further we dig into it, the bigger we think the opportunity is...The long-term goal is to build multiple multi-gig energy campuses."
  • Whitfield asked about the Bolt Energy partnership and potential water revenue. Glover said, "With certain designs, yes, those numbers are very reasonable...we think that this could be pretty material to the water business over the long term."
  • Whitfield followed up on record water volumes and source water realization. Robert Crain, Executive Vice President, explained, "The results you're seeing in those higher numbers is a result of those legacy contracts and our strategy that we've implemented."
  • Tim Rezvan, KeyBanc: Asked if TPL anticipates publishing more information on data center opportunities. Glover replied, "I think eventually, we'll put more information out...we do hope to put a stronger narrative out eventually."
  • Rezvan inquired about desalination efficiencies and power intensity. Crain answered, "The overall goal in water desal is to reduce energy consumption to overall reduce your price per barrel...anything we can do from a waste heat capture standpoint, waste heat is almost free energy."
  • Rezvan also asked about rare earth exploration on TPL acreage. Glover indicated there are "a couple of exploration projects going on currently...Early stages, but findings seem promising so far."
  • Hsu-Lei Huang, Tudor, Pickering, Holt & Co.: Sought details about supplying water for Bolt projects. Crain replied, "I would say both...it's going to probably be eventually a combination of both water sources."
  • Huang asked about the ability to ramp treated water capacity. Crain stated, "We're never going to have a feedstock shortage of water usage for power gen or compute...we're going to have to look at what the gen capacity is, what the water needs are and then design around that."
  • Huang followed up on commercial partnerships and anchor customers for Bolt. Glover said, "His goal is to get to 1 gig pretty swiftly with kind of a 10-gig campus being the ultimate goal...hopefully, we're announcing some stuff at least this year, if not first half of the year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for specifics on data center opportunities, water business segmentation, and commercialization timelines, displaying a slightly positive yet inquisitive tone driven by record performance and new initiatives.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing growth, strategic partnerships, and operational resilience. Glover said, "We are hopeful to have multiple new updates to share before the end of the year."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from cautious optimism about the macro environment to more enthusiastic confidence regarding next-generation growth avenues.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for capital expenditures in 2026 is consistent with the prior quarter's outlook, but newly disclosed allocations for data center and desalination co-location signal an increased focus on next-generation opportunities.
  • Management discussed a more advanced pipeline of data center projects, with urgency and customer engagement described as having "clearly increased" versus prior quarters.
  • Analysts in the current quarter focused more on scaling of new ventures and specific commercial milestones, diverging from the previous quarter's emphasis on M&A and traditional royalty growth.
  • Key metrics such as water sales and produced water royalty volumes set new records, building on strong sequential and year-over-year growth previously reported.
  • Management's confidence in technology-driven growth and strategic partnerships was notably stronger this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged challenges inherent to large-scale data center projects, including "extensive due diligence and negotiations across numerous counterparties."
  • Crain noted, "The overall goal in water desal is to reduce energy consumption to overall reduce your price per barrel," addressing power intensity concerns of desalination.
  • Industry-wide rig count declines and DUC drawdowns were discussed, but Steddum explained that operator efficiencies and longer well laterals are mitigating risks of production declines.
  • Analysts raised questions about feasibility and scalability of both water and data center opportunities, as well as early-stage rare earth exploration.

Final Takeaway

Texas Pacific Land Corporation closed 2025 with record operational and financial results, propelled by robust oil and gas royalty production and unprecedented water sales. Management spotlighted significant progress in data center partnerships and desalination technology, with concrete investments and multiple commercial negotiations underway. Despite lower oil prices, TPL reiterated its ability to invest and grow opportunistically, underpinned by a debt-free balance sheet and substantial liquidity. The company maintains that expanding into next-generation infrastructure, while continuing to set records in its core businesses, positions it for sustained value creation in 2026 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Baron Asset Fund adds Axon in Q4, sells Trade Desk
data.description:
• Baron Asset Fund gained 7.89% (institutional shares) in Q4, outperforming its benchmark Russell Midcap Growth Index.
  • The Russell Midcap Growth Index declined 3.70% in the quarter.
  • The fund reestablished a position in Repligen (RGEN).
  • Additionally, the fund added Booz Allen Hamilton (BAH), Birkenstock (BIRK), Welltower (WELL), and Axon Enterprise (AXON) to its portfolio during the quarter.
  • Baron Asset Fund sold Trade Desk (TTD), Gartner (IT), IDEXX Laboratories (IDXX), Dayforce (DAY), and IDEX (IEX) in Q4.
  • Source: Fund Letter

data.title: Glencore nears $4B sale of Kazakhstan zinc mining business - Bloomberg
data.description:
Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) is in talks to sell its stake in Kazzinc Ltd. in Kazakhstan to a local businessman, Bloomberg reported Thursday, as part of a series of deals that could reshape the country's mining landscape.

Shakhmurat Mutalip is in discussions to purchase the miner's 70% stake in zinc and gold producer Kazzinc in a deal that could be worth $4B-$4.5B, and Glencore may extend funding for the purchase, according to the report.

Separately, Mutalip also is said to be in talks to buy 40% of Eurasian Resources Group from the families of two of the Kazakh mining group's founders and is talking to both Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) and rival trader Mercuria about funding for that transaction, the report said.

Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) has held various talks in the past year about selling its holdings across Kazakhstan, which have helped underpin its decades-long dominance in the global zinc market; Kazzinc was one of the company's most profitable assets last year, thanks largely to surging prices for gold, which it mines as a byproduct.
